Inside the Algorithm: A Visual Story of Social Media Ads

Every time you open TikTok, Instagram, or YouTube, an algorithm decides which ads to show you. Over one session on 1 April 2026, I logged every ad I encountered — recording the platform, category, duration, skippability, and how relevant it felt. This is what I found.

20Ads logged
3Platforms
5Ad categories
~7 minSession length

TikTok dominates my ad feed

Of the 20 ads logged, 15 came from TikTok — that’s 75% of all ads seen in a single session. Instagram and YouTube each contributed just a handful. This reflects how much more time I spend scrolling TikTok compared to other platforms, but also how aggressively TikTok serves ads relative to content.

Key takeaway: TikTok’s ad frequency is strikingly high — I saw roughly one ad every 25–30 seconds of scrolling.

Health & fitness ads feel the most relevant — by far

Not all ad categories land equally. Health & fitness ads scored a mean relevance of 4.7 out of 5, far ahead of every other category. Tech/apps came second at 2.6, while fashion/beauty and food & drink both scored just 1 — the lowest possible. This suggests the algorithm has learned my interests well in at least one domain, even if it misses the mark in others.

Key takeaway: The algorithm seems to have a strong signal on health & fitness interest, but struggles to serve relevant fashion or food content.

15-second ads hit the sweet spot for relevance

Does the length of an ad affect how relevant it feels? Looking at mean relevance by duration, 15-second ads achieved the highest average score (~3.75), followed by a dip at 30 and 60 seconds. Interestingly, 0-second ads (instant-skip formats on Instagram) rated low, which may reflect that they’re served without any targeting signal at all.

Key takeaway: 15-second ads appear to be the optimal length — long enough to communicate, short enough not to overstay their welcome.

Ad frequency ramped up mid-session

Logging ads across the session revealed an interesting temporal pattern: ad frequency was relatively low at the start (7:07–7:09 pm), then surged between 7:11 and 7:12 pm, with 5 ads logged in a single minute. This could reflect the algorithm “warming up” as it gathers more behavioural data from the session, or simply a cluster of ad breaks in TikTok’s feed structure.

Key takeaway: Ad density isn’t constant — it appears to increase as a session progresses, possibly as the algorithm gathers more engagement data.

What does this tell us?

Across just 20 ads and 7 minutes of scrolling, a clear picture emerges: TikTok is the dominant ad platform, health & fitness content is highly targeted to my interests, and shorter ads tend to feel more relevant. The temporal data also hints that ad load isn’t uniform — it builds over a session.

What’s perhaps most striking is how uneven the relevance scores are. The algorithm clearly “knows” some things about me (my fitness interests) but completely misses others (food, fashion). This raises an interesting question: is the ad experience shaped more by what platforms know about us, or by the sheer volume of ads they serve?

Data collected via personal observational logging on 1 April 2026 · STATS 220 Project 4 · Data source: Google Form (Google Sheets published CSV)
